title: podman run --user adds user to their primary group in /etc/group
body:
### Issue Description

`podman run --user='1001:1001'` creates user `1001` with their primary group `1001` in `/etc/passwd` in container but also assigns same user to same group as secondary group in `/etc/group`.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue in Debian 13 (testing)

```root@d13:~# apt-get update && apt-get install podman

root@d13:~# dpkg -s podman | grep Version
Version: 5.4.0+ds1-1

root@d13:~# useradd test

root@d13:~# id test
uid=1001(test) gid=1001(test) grupy=1001(test)

root@d13:~# podman run --user="1001:1001" -t debian:stable tail -n1 /etc/passwd
test:*:1001:1001::/:/bin/sh

root@d13:~# podman run --user="1001:1001" -t debian:stable tail -n1 /etc/group
1001:x:1001:1001
```


### Describe the results you received

```
root@d13:~# podman run --user="1001:1001" -t debian:stable tail -n1 /etc/group
1001:x:1001:1001
```


### Describe the results you expected

```
root@d13:~# podman run --user="1001:1001" -t debian:stable tail -n1 /etc/group
1001:x:1001:
```


### podman info output

```yaml
5.4.0+ds1-1
```

### Podman in a container

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

Rootless

### Upstream Latest Release

Yes

### Additional environment details

Additional environment details

### Additional information

Additional information like issue happens only occasionally or issue happens with a particular architecture or on a particular setting

comments.body:
yes that is expected.

The GID duplicated in the additional groups is needed to avoid security issues like CVE-2022-2989
